items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: Does adding a catalyst change the value of Kc?
items.correct: No — a catalyst speeds up forward and reverse reactions equally, so Kc is unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst increases Kc by favouring product formation.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst decreases Kc by stabilising the reactants.
items.why: Students think catalysts 'help the reaction' so they must increase Kc; missing that Kc is determined by thermodynamics (ΔG), not by rate.

items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.
Correct answer: CN⁻. “H⁺” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Members of a homologous series share:
items.correct: The same functional group and general formula
items.why: It's The same functional group and general formula. “The same molecular mass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
